startScreenOptions.title: AN70.1-AN71.2 — Interactive Lecture (AN)
startScreenOptions.shortStartDescription: Interactive lecture with 3 embedded questions for AN70.1-AN71.2.

interactions.label: The parotid gland produces a watery, enzyme-rich secretion while the sublingual

params.question: The parotid gland produces a watery, enzyme-rich secretion while the sublingual gland produces viscous mucin. Which histological feature best explains this functional difference?

answers.text: The parotid has a branching compound duct system while the sublingual has simple unbranched ducts

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Both the parotid and sublingual are compound glands with branching duct systems. The key difference lies in their acinar cell types, not their duct architecture.

answers.text: The parotid contains serous acini with basophilic cytoplasm and round nuclei, while the sublingual contains mucous acini with pale, foamy cytoplasm

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Serous acini have pyramid-shaped cells with central round nuclei and basophilic cytoplasm packed with zymogen granules, producing enzyme-rich secretions. Mucous acini have columnar cells with flattened basal nuclei and pale cytoplasm, producing viscous mucin.

answers.text: The parotid uses holocrine secretion while the sublingual uses merocrine secretion

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Both salivary glands use merocrine secretion (exocytosis with the cell membrane intact). Holocrine secretion, where the entire cell disintegrates, is characteristic of sebaceous glands.

answers.text: The parotid has serous demilunes capping its acini while the sublingual does not

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Serous demilunes (Giannuzzi demilunes) are characteristic of the submandibular gland, a mixed gland. The purely serous parotid has no mucous acini to cap.

overallFeedback.feedback: Not quite. The critical histological distinction is in acinar cell type: serous acini (basophilic, enzyme-rich) versus mucous acini (pale, mucin-producing). This cellular difference directly explains why salivary glands produce different types of secretion.

Correct! The type of acinar cells — serous versus mucous — directly determines the nature of the gland's secretion. This structure-function correlation is the key to identifying salivary glands under the microscope.

interactions.label: The paracortex of a lymph node is rich in B lymphocytes and contains the germina

params.question: The paracortex of a lymph node is rich in B lymphocytes and contains the germinal centres where antibody-producing cells mature.

l10n.wrongAnswerMessage: Actually, the paracortex is the T cell zone, not the B cell zone. B cell follicles with germinal centres are found in the outer cortex. The paracortex contains T lymphocytes and high endothelial venules — the site where blood-borne lymphocytes re-enter the lymph node.
l10n.correctAnswerMessage: Correct! The paracortex is the T lymphocyte zone, containing T cells and high endothelial venules through which circulating lymphocytes enter the node from blood. B cell follicles and germinal centres are located in the outer cortex.

interactions.label: Which structural feature of fibrocartilage makes it particularly suited to withs

params.question: Which structural feature of fibrocartilage makes it particularly suited to withstand tensile stress in the intervertebral disc and knee meniscus?

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Elastic fibres are the defining feature of elastic cartilage (epiglottis, external ear), which is designed for flexible recoil rather than tensile resistance.

answers.text: A thick perichondrium that continuously regenerates chondrocytes

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Fibrocartilage is unique among cartilage types in lacking a perichondrium entirely. Hyaline and elastic cartilage possess a perichondrium, but it serves growth and nutrition rather than tensile strength.

answers.text: Dense bundles of type I collagen visible on routine histological staining

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Type I collagen bundles give fibrocartilage its tensile strength and are visible on H&E staining, unlike the type II collagen in hyaline cartilage which appears glassy. This is why fibrocartilage is found where cartilage must resist pulling forces.

answers.text: A glassy, homogeneous matrix of type II collagen

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: A glassy matrix of type II collagen is the hallmark of hyaline cartilage, which covers articular surfaces and forms the embryonic skeleton. Type II collagen is invisible on routine staining, giving hyaline cartilage its characteristic glassy appearance.

overallFeedback.feedback: Not quite. Fibrocartilage owes its tensile strength to dense bundles of type I collagen, visible on routine staining. This sets it apart from hyaline cartilage (type II collagen, glassy matrix) and elastic cartilage (elastic fibres for recoil).

Correct! Dense type I collagen bundles distinguish fibrocartilage from hyaline and elastic cartilage and directly account for its ability to resist the tensile forces in intervertebral discs and menisci.
